video_title: A Market Reversal Is Inevitable | Chance Finucane @OxbowAdvisors
gist: Oxbow Advisors CIO Chance Finucane tells Adam Taggart the AI/semiconductor trade shows bubble-level extremes (record momentum, concentration, and margin debt) and that a severe multi-year reversal is inevitable though untimeable; Oxbow keeps AI-beneficiary exposure near 5% of its growth portfolio, is trimming into strength and buying prior pullbacks (precious metals, energy, defensive 'halo trade' stocks), shortening bond duration toward a 30/30/30/10 framework, and counsels clients to preserve capital for the eventual buying opportunity.

insights.claim: The semiconductor index rose more than 230% over the trailing 14 months as of mid-2026, a magnitude matched only once before in the chart's history: the final 14 months of the dot-com bubble into March 2000.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Momentum this extreme has historically preceded severe, multi-year sector bear markets, flagging semiconductor exposure as a high-risk area to trim rather than chase.
insights.quote: In the last 14 months, the semiconductor index has appreciated by more than 230%, which has only happened one other time, which was the last 14 months of the dot-com bubble.

insights.claim: Momentum stocks have outperformed minimum-volatility stocks by the widest margin in 30 years of data, and the current overshoot versus trend is roughly a five-standard-deviation extreme, the most severe reading since 2000, surpassing even the 2007-08 housing bubble period.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A five-sigma overshoot implies an unusually large mean-reversion risk; investors overweight momentum names should expect an outsized retracement, not just a mild pullback.
insights.quote: Momentum stocks have outperformed minimum volatility stocks by more than any time in on record in the last 30 years.

insights.claim: AI-related stocks make up roughly 45% of S&P 500 market capitalization and about 70% of the Nasdaq 100, meaning a reversal in the AI trade would drag the broad indexes down along with many ETFs that hold these mega-cap tech names, including funds not typically thought of as tech-heavy.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Passive index and even non-tech ETF investors carry far more AI-trade concentration risk than they may realize, warranting a look under the hood of holdings.
insights.quote: 45% of the S&P market cap is made up of AI related stocks.

insights.claim: In June 2026, the semiconductor index moved more than 5% in a single day on roughly half of all trading days, a level of daily volatility Chance Finucane says cannot reflect genuine changes in underlying business value and instead signals extreme speculation.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Volatility of this magnitude in supposedly fundamentals-driven mega-cap names is itself a late-cycle speculation indicator worth monitoring for further deterioration.
insights.quote: essentially half the trading days in the month of June, the semiconductor index went up or down by more than 5%.

insights.claim: IBM, a roughly quarter-trillion-dollar market-cap 'blue chip,' dropped about 25% in a single trading day, which Adam Taggart cites alongside semiconductor moves as evidence of manic, late-stage-bubble-like volatility spreading beyond purely speculative names.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Large single-day drops in traditionally stable blue-chip names suggest fragility is broadening beyond the most speculative corners of the market.
insights.quote: IBM stock opened today and was down basically 25%.

insights.claim: When a sector goes through a bubble-type move of hundreds of percent in a short period, Oxbow's research (using Cisco Systems and Nvidia during the dot-com era as examples) finds it typically takes about two and a half years after the peak to reach a new bottom.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: If the semiconductor/AI cycle peaks, investors should plan for a prolonged, multi-year drawdown rather than a quick V-shaped recovery before re-entering.
insights.quote: it tends to be about two and a half years that you go down until you make a new bottom.

insights.claim: A 40-50% overall market decline would imply the prior cycle's biggest winners fall by two-thirds or more from their peaks, given how concentrated recent gains have been.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Investors overweight the current cycle's leaders should size position risk assuming downside far steeper than the headline index decline.
insights.quote: if you have a a 40% or 50% market decline, that means the winners of the last cycle are probably going down by 2/3 or more.

insights.claim: During the 2000-2003 dot-com bear market, tech stocks and the broad index fell sharply, but investors could still make money because a number of previously sold-off sectors from the late 1990s outperformed; Oxbow is trying to identify today's equivalent unloved sectors ahead of a similar rotation.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A broad AI-trade unwind need not mean a total portfolio loss; capital rotation into unloved, undervalued sectors historically offers a place to hide and even profit.

insights.claim: The current secular bull market since the 2009 financial-crisis low is about 17 years old, and historical secular bull runs tend to last roughly 20 years, implying limited further runway before a regime shift comparable to the stagnant 1968-1982 stock market.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Investors, especially those near retirement who have benefited from 17 years of gains, should weigh the historical odds that the secular bull is in its final stretch rather than assume indefinite continuation.

insights.claim: Tech analyst Fred Hickey argues hyperscaler earnings are artificially inflated by one-time investment gains tied to stakes in frontier-model companies like OpenAI and Anthropic, which are not recurring operating profits.
insights.speaker: Fred Hickey (relayed by Adam Taggart)
insights.so_what: If reported hyperscaler earnings are overstated by non-operating gains, headline valuation multiples understate true overvaluation, making the sector riskier than it appears.
insights.quote: these earnings are getting boosted by a lot of one-time gains in investments that these companies have made like in a lot of the frontier models like Open AI or Anthropic.

insights.claim: On a cyclically-adjusted (CAPE-style) basis, Fred Hickey puts the AI/hyperscaler sector's valuation near 67 times earnings, far above the seemingly reasonable multiples implied by headline current-year P/E figures.
insights.speaker: Fred Hickey (relayed by Adam Taggart)
insights.so_what: Investors relying on headline PE ratios to justify AI valuations may be missing a much richer adjusted multiple that signals significant overvaluation.
insights.quote: he says it's more like 67 times.

insights.claim: Chance Finucane describes an accounting dynamic where hyperscaler AI capex is recognized as 100% revenue immediately by the semiconductor and capital-equipment companies selling the gear, while on the hyperscalers' own books that same spending is only expensed at roughly 20% per year over a five-year depreciation schedule, meaning today's reported hyperscaler profitability outpaces the true economics once depreciation catches up.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: As depreciation expense compounds in future years, hyperscaler margins could compress meaningfully even without revenue declining, a headwind not yet priced into current earnings estimates.
insights.quote: it's it's 100% of the revenue being recognized today, but then on the expense line, uh since you can depreciate it, let's just say over five years, only 20% of that is being expensed right now.

insights.claim: Semiconductor and capital-equipment companies have captured nearly all of the hyperscalers' free cash flow over the past couple of years, a dynamic Chance Finucane doubts hyperscaler management teams -- among the smartest in the world -- will tolerate indefinitely, making a pushback on AI spending a plausible catalyst for a reversion in the trade.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Watch for hyperscalers slowing capex growth or seeking efficiency gains as an early signal that the semiconductor trade's free-cash-flow capture is ending.
insights.quote: the semiconductor businesses have basically stolen all of the free cash flow from the hyperscalers uh in the last couple of years.

insights.claim: Chamath Palihapitiya's CTO reported that the cost of AI tokens for his company was doubling roughly every 45 days while measured productivity gains from that spend were only about 5%, prompting Chamath and, per Fred Hickey, other companies to start ratcheting down token and compute usage.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya (anecdote relayed by Fred Hickey via Adam Taggart)
insights.so_what: Deteriorating unit economics of AI compute spend among sophisticated AI-bullish operators is an early warning sign for slowing enterprise AI capex and token-consumption growth.
insights.quote: the cost of the our tokens is doubling like every 45 days.

insights.claim: Some large hyperscalers, including Microsoft, are reportedly shifting workloads to cheaper Chinese AI models that deliver roughly 90% of needed task performance at a fraction of what OpenAI or Anthropic charge, raising national-security and corporate-security concerns that strengthen the case for cybersecurity investment.
insights.speaker: Fred Hickey (relayed by Adam Taggart)
insights.so_what: A shift toward cheaper Chinese AI models both pressures the pricing power of US frontier-model providers and creates a durable tailwind for cybersecurity spending.
insights.quote: you can still get about 90% of the the tasks you want done but just at a fraction of what Anthropic or OpenAI might charge you.

insights.claim: A bullish sell-side analyst's own model shows Micron's earnings per share surging to $250 by 2028 (with gross margin doubling to roughly 90% from a normal ~45%) before collapsing 80% to about $50 by 2030 as the memory chip cycle turns, implying a base-case share price near $400 -- about two-thirds below its recent $1,200 peak -- once Micron reverts to its normal ~8x earnings multiple on normalized profits.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Even the bull case for a hot AI-supply-chain name like Micron embeds a severe subsequent earnings collapse, illustrating why Oxbow avoids the most cyclical AI beneficiaries despite currently strong reported profits.
insights.quote: by 2030, that same bullish analyst, just knowing the cycles of memory chips, has the earnings per share dropping from $250 in 2028 to 50. An 80% decline in earnings in 2 years.

insights.claim: Oxbow's overall split between risky assets and short-term Treasuries has stayed roughly stable through 2026; the active management has instead come from trimming positions after they become 'too exciting' and valuations can't be justified, then buying back in after a pullback of 30-40-50%.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A disciplined trim-into-strength, buy-the-drawdown process is a repeatable framework retail investors can apply instead of trying to time absolute market tops and bottoms.
insights.quote: We're usually trimming, taking some money out of those areas just because we think it's you know, it's gotten too exciting in the market.

insights.claim: Oxbow trimmed precious metals positions when gold traded above $5,000/oz and silver was over $100-120/oz in January 2026, and has spent the last month rebuilding those positions now that gold is back around $4,000 and silver around $60, levels the firm had targeted in advance.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Illustrates a concrete, price-target-based rebalancing discipline in precious metals that investors could mirror rather than chasing or panic-selling gold and silver.
insights.quote: gold is back around 4,000, silver is back around 60, those were always kind of our targets

insights.claim: Oxbow trimmed energy exposure when oil prices spiked unexpectedly amid the war in Iran in March 2026, then added back after oil subsequently fell about 40% and energy stocks pulled back.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Shows the same trim-on-spike, buy-on-drawdown discipline applied to energy, a sector Oxbow is using as a diversifier away from the AI trade.

insights.claim: In January-February 2026, a 'halo trade' into heavy-asset, low-obsolescence-risk stocks (industrials, consumer staples, healthcare, utilities) bid those names up as an escape from AI-disruption risk; when the Iran war caused a March selloff in those same defensive sectors, Oxbow bought the dip, and those positions have rallied for months, accelerating as capital rotates out of the AI trade over the last three weeks.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Defensive, low-AI-disruption-risk sectors are already showing early rotation flows out of AI and into value, potentially the leading edge of the broader unwind Oxbow expects.
insights.quote: the whole first 2 months of this year was that halo trade, the heavy asset, low obsolescence uh stock trade to get away from disrupted AI stocks

insights.claim: AI-beneficiary positions make up only about 5% of Oxbow's long-term growth portfolio; the firm bought cybersecurity company Fortinet in January 2026 at roughly 20 times free cash flow when cyber stocks were mistakenly grouped with AI-disruption risks, the stock has since doubled to about 40 times free cash flow (near a 6-7 year high multiple), and Oxbow cut the position in half to bank gains and remove its cost basis.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A real, dated example of Oxbow's buy-cheap/trim-rich discipline: entering an unloved beneficiary sector at a low multiple and systematically taking profits once valuation re-rates to a multi-year high.
insights.quote: we had bought Fortinet at a little over 20 times free cash flow. And it's now doubled in 6 months

insights.claim: Oxbow's recent long-term growth additions -- IDEXX (niche industrial products with high customer switching costs and pricing power), McKesson (drug distributor benefiting from GLP-1 weight-loss drug distribution), and US Bancorp (a top-quality super-regional bank) -- reflect rotation into industrials, healthcare, and financials rather than chasing the AI trade.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Provides investors specific, named alternatives to AI mega-caps that a high-net-worth advisory firm is currently buying as 'unloved' quality names.

insights.claim: In its high-income strategy, Oxbow has been adding Northern Oil and Gas and Kimbell Royalty as oil prices fell back below roughly $70-80, and rebuilding precious metals, gold/silver miners, and royalty positions back to target base allocations.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Gives income-focused investors concrete names being accumulated as a diversification away from AI/semiconductor concentration.

insights.claim: Among US households that own financial assets, roughly 73% of those assets are held in stocks versus just 7% in bonds -- a record equity concentration -- and stock exposure is highest among households approaching or in retirement, the group with the least time to recover from a severe drawdown.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: A severe AI-trade unwind would disproportionately harm near-retirement and retired households who are both the most equity-exposed and least able to wait out a multi-year recovery.
insights.quote: they're 73% in stocks and 7% uh in bonds

insights.claim: Margin debt relative to money supply is at its highest ratio on record; per data from David Rosenberg, two-thirds of all outstanding margin debt has been added in just the last six years (roughly tripling), and margin debt is up more than 50% year-over-year, a surge that has occurred only three other times in 30 years -- early 2000, mid-2007, and early 2021 -- each preceding a bear market.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Record margin debt growth is a recurring precursor to major bear markets (dot-com, GFC, 2022), making current leverage levels a systemic risk gauge worth tracking for a forced-deleveraging trigger.
insights.quote: 2/3 of all margin debt uh has come on in the last 6 years, uh which is pretty staggering that it's essentially tripled uh in a 6-year period

insights.claim: SpaceX's opening-day IPO price valued it at roughly 79 times sales, or about 58 times next year's projected revenue -- among the most excited IPO demand Oxbow founder Ted Oakley has seen from clients calling in during his career -- compared to Google's 2004 IPO, which priced at about 8.5 times revenue.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Today's 'reasonable premium' for a hot tech IPO is many multiples richer than historical comparables like Google's IPO, signaling extreme investor demand that has historically not persisted.
insights.quote: that opening price you saw on the first day it was trading at 58 times next year's revenue.

insights.claim: Looking at the last decade of hyped IPOs, every one has at some point traded below its first-day closing price, typically cutting in half within the first year; SpaceX is already down about 12% from its first-day close.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Investors chasing newly public 'hot' names at IPO pricing have a historically poor entry-point track record; patience through the first year has repeatedly offered better entry prices.
insights.quote: the average usually is it cuts in half at some point during the first year.

insights.claim: Oxbow waited roughly four to five years after Airbnb's 2020 IPO (around $145/share) before buying the stock at a little above $120/share once its valuation became more reasonable.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A concrete, multi-year patience example showing that waiting out post-IPO hype can produce a better entry price even in a genuinely high-quality company.
insights.quote: we watched it for four or five years uh, before we ended up taking a position at a little above $120 a share uh, when the valuation got more reasonable

insights.claim: Oxbow has moved its target portfolio framework away from the traditional 60/40 stocks/bonds split toward roughly 30/30/30/10 across short-duration high-quality fixed income, commodities (energy, precious metals, agriculture), high-quality stocks, and an opportunistic/special-situations sleeve that can include cash.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete alternative allocation framework for investors questioning whether the classic 60/40 model still fits a higher-inflation, more volatile regime.

insights.claim: Oxbow keeps fixed-income duration generally at 3 years or less and has locked in about 15% of its income-strategy portfolios in 2-3 year Treasuries yielding just above 4% (around 4.15%), betting that Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's inflation-fighting stance combined with favorable year-over-year base effects (from March's Iran-war oil spike rolling off) could bring inflation back toward 2% and trigger rate cuts within about a year.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Locking in short-duration yields near 4% ahead of anticipated Fed cuts is a specific, actionable fixed-income trade being deployed today.
insights.quote: It's now about 15% of those portfolios is locked in at rates of 4% or a little above a 4% yield.

insights.claim: Oxbow avoids long-duration Treasuries, viewing the post-2020 regime of higher and more volatile inflation combined with a widening US fiscal deficit, rising debt levels, and rising mandatory spending and interest expense as structural forces likely to keep long-term yields elevated.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Structural fiscal pressures argue against a long-duration Treasury bet even if short rates fall, a relevant caution for bond investors positioning for rate cuts.

insights.claim: As an example of Oxbow's opportunistic sleeve, the firm bought Boeing convertible preferred stock a couple of years ago for its attractive yield while the common share price had fallen significantly, planning to eventually shift into the common stock at an appreciated price.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Illustrates a specific special-situations technique -- buying convertible preferred shares of a good business in temporary distress -- for generating yield with upside optionality.
insights.quote: Boeing issued a convertible preferred stock that had a good yield and the common share price had really gone down a lot

themes.theme: Wealth Management Practice for High-Net-Worth Clients

insights.claim: For clients experiencing a sudden liquidity event (business sale, concentrated stock windfall, inheritance), Oxbow's default advice is that there is no rush to deploy the capital -- park it in T-bills near a risk-free ~4% yield and take up to a year to adjust to the life change before committing to a portfolio.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Counters the pressure many new advisory clients feel to deploy capital immediately; parking in T-bills preserves optionality without sacrificing meaningful yield while decisions are made.
insights.quote: there's no rush to take action with this liquidity event, this cash flow that you just received.

insights.claim: Oxbow's own historical data showed that slowly dollar-cost-averaging new client money into stocks underperformed versus immediately sizing an appropriately risk-calibrated equity sleeve (currently about 60% stocks/40% Treasuries in the long-term growth strategy, using reasonably-valued rather than overvalued names) and deploying it within the first few months.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Challenges the common assumption that slow-drip investing is always the safer onboarding approach; Oxbow's data favors calibrated but prompt deployment over gradual dollar-cost averaging.
insights.quote: we used to buy in, for example, in the stock strategy, really slowly. And we actually have uh enough data to suggest that um that actually was not helping our clients as much as it could have

insights.claim: For clients transitioning large, highly appreciated concentrated positions into Oxbow's strategy, the firm caps realized taxable gains at roughly 10% of portfolio value per year (about 2% of the portfolio paid in long-term capital gains tax), migrating the account over two to three years rather than triggering a large one-time tax bill.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, replicable rule of thumb (about 10% of portfolio value in gains, ~2% in tax per year) for tax-efficiently unwinding concentrated stock positions.
insights.quote: if you had a a portfolio that was doubling every 5 years, um and you decided you needed to sell uh 1/5 of the portfolio per year, you would essentially be recognizing 10% of the portfolio uh in gains and paying about 2% of the portfolio in taxes.

insights.claim: Oxbow Advisors' minimum account threshold to become a client is $2 million in investable assets.
insights.speaker: Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Sets clear expectations for which investors can directly engage Oxbow versus needing to self-manage using the firm's publicly shared philosophy and trades.
insights.quote: the minimum we would set would be 2 million.
